nativeRange.habitat: The Japanese Pinstripe Basslet is a reef-associated species, typically reported from caves and crevices on lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of roughly 2–34 m.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty frozen foods including mysis shrimp and vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and has been reported to take high-quality pellets and flakes once acclimated. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly advised. Newly imported specimens are often shy and may need targeted feeding near their cave until they learn to come out at mealtime.

avoidWith: predators that may harass shy fish
ornamental shrimp small enough to be eaten

summary: A small, cryptic Indo-Pacific reef basslet with bright orange-red flanks marked by thin dark pinstripes. Reclusive cave-dweller, generally considered reef-safe, and best kept singly with plenty of rockwork.
careNotes: The Japanese Pinstripe Basslet (Liopropoma susumi) is a small, secretive reef basslet with a wide Indo-Pacific range — from the Red Sea east to the Line Islands and Samoa, north to the Ryukyu Islands of Japan, and south to Reunion, New Caledonia, and the Australian reefs. In the wild it occupies caves, crevices, and overhangs on lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of roughly 2–34 m. The captive aquarium should mirror this, with plenty of live rock providing deep caves and coral overhangs, low to moderate flow, and a relatively undisturbed mid-to-lower water column. Like other Liopropoma species, individuals tend to spend much of their time near or inside their chosen cave, especially in the first weeks after introduction, and often become more visible once they settle into the feeding routine. The Japanese Pinstripe Basslet is generally considered reef-safe with corals, though small ornamental shrimp and other tiny crustaceans within mouth-gape are at risk. It is best kept as a single specimen per tank, as conspecific aggression tends to be high and pairs are very difficult to establish outside very large systems. Members of the basslet family have been known to jump, so a tightly fitted lid is strongly advised.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Liopropoma susumi has not been documented in the hobbyist literature. Sex-determination and reproductive mode for this species are poorly studied; one retail source characterizes it as gonochoristic, but published peer-reviewed confirmation is lacking.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Used as primary because the assigned LiveAquaria URL (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/japanese-pinstripe-basslet) returned HTTP 404. FishBase provided family/order, distribution, depth range (2–34 m), max size (9.1 cm TL), preferred temperature (25.3–29.3°C, mean 28.3°C), trophic level, and behavior (secretive, cave/crevice-dwelling on lagoon and seaward reefs).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-source for aquarium husbandry: max size 3.5 in (~8.9 cm), minimum tank 50 gal (~190 L), care level beginner, temperament peaceful, reef-compatible yes, diet carnivore. Confirmed trade names Meteor Basslet, Meteor Perch, Striped Basslet, Susumi Basslet.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-source for water parameters: temp 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12; minimum tank 30 gal (~114 L); max size 4 in (~10 cm); care level moderate; reef-safe yes; jumper (lid recommended); best kept singly; characterized as gonochoristic. Temperature spread vs FishBase (25.3–29.3°C wild vs QM 22.2–25.6°C captive) was reconciled by adopting a synthesized 24–27°C range that overlaps both. Tank-size spread (30–50 gal across retailers) was reconciled at 150 L (~40 gal) biased toward the larger figure because of the species' cave-territory needs and shy demeanor.

additional.name: Fishes of Australia
additional.notes: Independent biological confirmation: family Serranidae (older classification; modern usage Liopropomatidae), reef-associated, depth to ~30 m, max ~9 cm TL, Australian distribution (Western Australia, Great Barrier Reef, Christmas Island, Middleton Reef) consistent with the broader Indo-Pacific range.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented for this species in the hobbyist literature.
